title: Geometry
blurb: Unit-by-unit practice for the Geometry course — from points and angles to circles, area, and coordinates.

units.title: Foundations: Points, Lines & Angles
units.blurb: Points, lines, rays, segments and planes; measuring segments and angles; angle types, angle pairs, and bisectors.

units.title: Reasoning & Proof
units.blurb: From noticing patterns to proving facts: conjectures, if-then statements, and your first two-column proofs.

units.title: Parallel & Perpendicular Lines
units.blurb: Transversals and the angle pairs they create — which are congruent, which are supplementary — and proving lines parallel.

units.title: Triangles & Congruence
units.blurb: Triangle types, the angle sum, the triangle inequality, and the congruence shortcuts SSS · SAS · ASA · AAS · HL.

units.title: Relationships in Triangles
units.blurb: Bisectors, medians, altitudes, the midsegment theorem, and inequalities inside a triangle.

units.title: Quadrilaterals & Polygons
units.blurb: Angle sums for any polygon; parallelograms, rectangles, rhombi, squares, trapezoids, and kites — with proofs.

units.title: Similarity
units.blurb: Ratio and proportion, similar polygons, the AA · SSS · SAS similarity shortcuts, and the side-splitter theorem.

units.title: Right Triangles & Trigonometry
units.blurb: The Pythagorean theorem, special right triangles, and sine · cosine · tangent with elevation and depression.

units.title: Circles
units.blurb: Radius, chord, tangent, secant; central and inscribed angles; arcs; and the circle relationships.

units.title: Area & Perimeter
units.blurb: Areas of triangles, quadrilaterals and regular polygons; circumference, circle area, sectors, and composite figures.

units.title: Surface Area & Volume
units.blurb: Prisms, cylinders, pyramids, cones, and spheres — wrapping and filling 3-D solids.

units.title: Transformations
units.blurb: Translations, reflections, rotations, and dilations; symmetry; congruence and similarity re-seen through motion.

units.title: Coordinate Geometry
units.blurb: Distance, midpoint, and slope; equations of lines and circles; proofs with coordinates — the course capstone.

variants.statement: Ray $OB$ bisects $\angle AOC$, and $\angle AOC = 40°$. Find $\angle AOB$.
variants.explanation: A bisector cuts the angle into two equal halves: $\angle AOB = 40° / 2 = 20°$. Leaving it as $40°$ forgets that $AOB$ is only half of $AOC$.

variants.statement: Two angles are complementary. One of them measures $5°$. Find the other angle.
variants.explanation: Complementary angles add to $90°$, so the other is $90° - 5° = 85°$. Subtracting from $180°$ (supplementary) is the classic mix-up, giving $175°$.

variants.statement: On a number line, $A = -3$ and $B = 9$. Find the coordinate of the midpoint of segment $AB$.
variants.explanation: Average the two coordinates: $\frac{(-3) + (9)}{2} = 3$. Adding without dividing by $2$ — the classic slip — gives $6$.

variants.statement: On a number line, $A = -8$ and $B = 2$. Find the coordinate of the midpoint of segment $AB$.

variants.statement: $B$ lies on segment $AC$ with $AB = 2$ and $BC = 7$. Find $AC$.
variants.explanation: Since $B$ is between $A$ and $C$, the whole is the sum of the parts: $AC = 2 + 7 = 9$. Subtracting to get $5$ is the classic slip — subtraction is only for finding a missing part.

variants.statement: What is the measure of one exterior angle of a regular $24$-gon?
variants.explanation: The exterior angles of any convex polygon add to $360°$, so each is $\frac{360°}{24} = 15°$. $165°$ is the interior angle — the classic swap.

variants.statement: Two similar triangles have similarity ratio $1 : 2$ (smaller to larger). The smaller triangle has perimeter $9$. Find the perimeter of the larger triangle.
variants.explanation: Perimeter is a LENGTH, so it scales by the plain ratio: $9 \cdot \frac{2}{1} = 18$. Scaling by the squared ratio (giving $36$) is correct for area, not perimeter — the classic mix-up.

forms.title: Similar triangles
forms.skill: Corresponding sides share one multiplier k — never an added amount.

variants.statement: Two similar triangles: the first has sides $2$ and $3$; the side of the second corresponding to $2$ is $4$. Find the side corresponding to $3$.
variants.explanation: Scale factor $k = \frac{4}{2}$. Corresponding sides multiply: $3\cdot\frac{4}{2} = 6$. Adding $2$ (the difference) is the classic wrong move — similarity multiplies, never adds.

variants.statement: Two similar triangles have similarity ratio $1 : 2$ (smaller to larger). The smaller triangle has area $2$. Find the area of the larger triangle.

variants.statement: A right triangle has hypotenuse $5$ and one leg $3$. Find the other leg.
variants.explanation: Subtract SQUARES, then take the root: $\sqrt{5^2 - 3^2} = 4$. Subtracting the lengths themselves — the classic slip — gives $2$.

forms.title: Reading trig ratios from a right triangle

variants.statement: A right triangle has legs $3$ and $4$ and hypotenuse $5$. What is $\sin$ of the acute angle opposite the side of length $3$?
variants.explanation: $\sin$ is opposite leg over hypotenuse: $\frac{3}{5}$. Swapping in the other leg (or inverting the fraction) is the classic mix-up.

forms.title: Circle area & circumference
forms.skill: A = πr² (fills), C = 2πr (wraps) — don't swap them.

variants.statement: A circle has radius $2$. Find its area, exactly.
variants.explanation: $A = \pi r^2 = \pi\cdot 2^2 = 4\pi$. ($2\pi r = 4\pi$ is the circumference, not the area.)

forms.title: Arc length from a degree measure

variants.statement: A circle has radius $6$. Find the length of an arc with a central angle of $60°$.
variants.explanation: Take the fraction of the full circumference: $\frac{60}{360} \cdot 2\pi \cdot 6 = 2\pi$. Multiplying by $\pi r^2$ instead of $2\pi r$ — the classic slip — computes a sector area, not a length.

variants.statement: A central angle of a circle measures $44°$. Find the measure of an inscribed angle that subtends the same arc.
variants.explanation: An inscribed angle is HALF the central angle on the same arc: $44° / 2 = 22°$. Keeping it equal at $44°$ is the classic slip.

variants.statement: A parallelogram has base $8$ and height $5$. Find its area.

variants.statement: An L-shaped region is a $10 \times 8$ rectangle with a $4 \times 3$ rectangle removed from one corner. Find the area of the L-shape.

variants.statement: The point $(3; -4)$ is translated by the vector $(2; 5)$. Find the image.
variants.explanation: Add the vector to each coordinate: $(3 + 2; -4 + 5) = (5; 1)$. Subtracting the vector moves the point the wrong way.

variants.statement: A translation maps $(3; -4)$ to $(5; 1)$. Which rule describes this translation?
variants.explanation: Subtract image minus preimage: the shift is $(2; 5)$, so the rule is $(x + 2;\ y + 5)$. Subtracting in the reverse order — the classic slip — flips both signs.

variants.statement: $M$ is the midpoint of segment $AB$. If $A = (2; 3)$ and $M = (5; -1)$, find $B$.
variants.explanation: Double the midpoint, then subtract $A$: $B = (2 \cdot 5 - 2;\ 2 \cdot -1 - 3) = (8; -5)$. Doubling $A$ instead of $M$ is the classic slip.
